coredata.dc:title: PAMAM dendrimer and carbon quantum dots complexes as theranostic nanocarrier: Synthesis, optimization and photophysical characterization
coredata.dc:creator: Sonam, Stanzin
Patel, Parth
Jain, Keerti
coredata.dc:description: The current research work proposes the optimization of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and photophysical characterization of its theranostic nanocomplexes with PAMAM dendrimers. In recent years, theranostic nanocarriers have been emerged as a promising nanocarrier for personalized medicine. The half generation (G3.5 and G4.5) and full generation (G4.0 and 5.0) PAMAM dendrimer nanocomplexes are comparatively less explored as theranostic nanocarrier, thus to study the photophysical behavior of these complexes in biological environment we have planned various studies at different conditions of pH, salt, temperature and radiation. The amphiphilic CQDs were synthesized using a facile microwave assisted pyrolysis method and optimized for various parameters to get highly fluorescent CQDs. Further, we have prepared CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer nanocomplexes via electrostatic interaction, which was confirmed by change in the emission maxima of CQDs and FTIR spectroscopy. The nanocomplexes showed 37.32 ± 4.57 % fluorescent quenching upon increasing the temperature 20 °C to 60 °C. The photostability study showed enhanced photobleaching upon increasing the generation (G4.0 to G5.0) due to structural changes, although it was significantly lower than organic dyes. The stability study of nanocomplexes showed suitable fluorescence intensity after 3 months at −20 ± 0.5 °C. Overall, these nanocomplexes holds great potential to serve as theranostic nanocarrier, thus offering an indispensable platform for personalized medicine in various aspects of cancer treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PAMAM dendrimer
Carbon quantum dots
Stability study
Photophysical characterization
Theranostic nanocarrier

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: 'Special section on All-optical simultaneous multiparameter sensing systems' edited by Dr. Alberto Sinibaldi, Dr. Carlos Marques

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential measurements
Effect of pH on fluorescence properties of CQDs and CQDs PAMAM complexes
Effect of ionic strength on fluorescence properties of CQDs and CQDs PAMAM complexes
Preparation of CQDs and PAMAM dendrimer complexes
Surface group analysis of CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM complexes by FTIR spectroscopy
Effect of temperature on fluorescence properties of CQDs and its complexes
Effect of pH on fluorescence behavior and stability of CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes
Effect of ionic strength on CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM complexes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Pharmaceuticals, Ministry of Chemicals and Fertilizers, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Department of Pharmaceuticals, Ministry of Chemicals and Fertilizers, Government of India for providing facilities to carry out this research work. The Authors also acknowledge SAIF, Panjab University for HRTEM analysis. The NIPER-R communication number for this manuscript is NIPER-R/Communication/453.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimization of various parameters to produce CQDs, reaction temperature (A); volume of reaction mixture (B); concentration of reaction mixture (C) and reaction time (D). (Note. “*****” signify the p value < 0.0001).
CQDs excitation wavelengths of 310–380 nm (A); excitation and emission spectra of CQDs (B); and CQDs images under daylight and UV light (C).

Intensity size distribution curve of CQDs (A); volume size distribution curve of CQDs dispersed in triple distilled water (B); zeta potential curve of CQDs (C); and average particle size in HRTEM images 12.34 ± 2.05 nm (D).
Fluorescence emission (λex = 345 nm) spectra of CQDs and CQD-dendrimers complexes (A) and images of CQDs-PAMAM dendrimers complexes under natural light and UV light (B).
FTIR Spectra of Citric acid, β-alanine and CQDs (A); G3.5, G4.5, G4.0 and G5.0 PAMAM dendrimers (B) and CQDs-PAMAM complexes (C).
Temperature dependent quenching studies of CQDs (A); CQDs-PAMAM G-3.5 complexes (B); CQDs-PAMAM G-4.5 complexes (C); CQDs-PAMAM G-4.0 complexes (D) and CQDs-PAMAM G-5.0 complexes (E).
Effect of pH on PL intensity of CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM complexes.
Effect of ionic strength on PL intensity of CQDs-PAMAM complexes in NaCl (A) and KCl solutions (B) in concentration range of 0–200 mM.
Effect of UV irradiation on fluorescence intensity of CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM complexes.
Storage stability of CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM complexes at −20 °C.
List of process and material attributes and its level.

head.ce:title: PAMAM dendrimer and carbon quantum dots complexes as theranostic nanocarrier: Synthesis, optimization and photophysical characterization

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Delivery and Nanomedicine Research Laboratory, Department of Pharmaceutics, National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research (NIPER) - Raebareli, Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Drug Delivery and Nanomedicine Research Laboratory
National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research (NIPER) - Raebareli

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Drug Delivery and Nanomedicine Research Laboratory, Department of Pharmaceutics, National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research (NIPER) - Raebareli, Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The current research work proposes the optimization of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and photophysical characterization of its theranostic nanocomplexes with PAMAM dendrimers. In recent years, theranostic nanocarriers have been emerged as a promising nanocarrier for personalized medicine. The half generation (G3.5 and G4.5) and full generation (G4.0 and 5.0) PAMAM dendrimer nanocomplexes are comparatively less explored as theranostic nanocarrier, thus to study the photophysical behavior of these complexes in biological environment we have planned various studies at different conditions of pH, salt, temperature and radiation. The amphiphilic CQDs were synthesized using a facile microwave assisted pyrolysis method and optimized for various parameters to get highly fluorescent CQDs. Further, we have prepared CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer nanocomplexes via electrostatic interaction, which was confirmed by change in the emission maxima of CQDs and FTIR spectroscopy. The nanocomplexes showed 37.32 ± 4.57 % fluorescent quenching upon increasing the temperature 20 °C to 60 °C. The photostability study showed enhanced photobleaching upon increasing the generation (G4.0 to G5.0) due to structural changes, although it was significantly lower than organic dyes. The stability study of nanocomplexes showed suitable fluorescence intensity after 3 months at −20 ± 0.5 °C. Overall, these nanocomplexes holds great potential to serve as theranostic nanocarrier, thus offering an indispensable platform for personalized medicine in various aspects of cancer treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, nanomaterial-based theranostic agents have emerged as a promising approach for simultaneous drug delivery and diagnosis [1,2]. Particularly, the chronic diseases like cancer require more detailed information after each chemo cycle to decide the dose of chemotherapy. Advancements in nano technology has provided alternative imaging agents, which are less toxic and have better fluorescence properties [3,4]. Theranostic nanocarriers could emerge as future of medicine, as it holds immense potential to treat chronic diseases [5]. In current time, various theranostic nanocarriers are widely explored by scientific community particularly for treatment of cancer [6]. Although, full and half generation dendrimer based nanotheranostic agents are comparatively less explored on one platform, thus in current research work we have performed various quenching, photostability and storage stability studies for nanocomplexes of dendrimer and carbon quantum dots (CQDs).

CQDs have been widely investigated due to their tunable size, excellent biocompatibility, photostability and excitation dependent fluorescence properties, which enable their use as bioimaging agents as well as drug delivery agents [7]. CQDs being fluorescent nanoparticles have unique optical, electronic and surface properties, which have various applications in bioimaging [8], biosensing [9] and delivery of various therapeutic agents [10,11]. CQDs consist of different surface functional groups, which provide better opportunity for surface modification with drugs or ligands and are considered as a potential candidate for designing of theranostic agents, which could be simultaneously explored for delivery of various drugs including anticancer [12], anti-Alzheimer [13] and anti-viral drugs [14]. In present research work, amphiphilic CQDs were produced using citric acid as carbon precursor and β-alanine as a surface passivating agent through microwave-assisted pyrolysis method.

Polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimers are synthetic and only commercialized polymers having uniform nanometric size range (3–6 nm) and a tree like structure. The interstitial void and cavities of dendrimers provide higher drug loading efficiency (more than 20 % in G3 and even greater in higher generation of dendrimers) make them superior nanocarrier compared to other polymeric and lipidic NPs [15,16]. Commercially available PAMAM dendrimers can be broadly classified into two categories: amine terminated (or full generation) dendrimers and carboxyl terminated (or half generation) dendrimers. The cationic charge on the peripheral sites of full generation PAMAM dendrimers facilitate enhanced interaction with the negatively charged cell membrane and facilitate cellular uptake of nanocarriers. Furthermore, free amine and carboxyl groups present on the surface of PAMAM dendrimers make them suitable candidate for surface functionalization either by chemical conjugation or electrostatic interactions [17,18]. Additionally, PAMAM dendrimers based theranostic agent could have many advantages like high drug loading, easy surface functionalization, etc., yet PAMAM dendrimer (a polymeric nanocarrier) based theranostic agents are comparatively less explored.

There are numerous imaging agents explored by scientific community. Amongst them, we have chosen the CQDs, because it has better biocompatibility compared to other imaging agents like carbon nanotubes and silicone based light emitting devices [19]. Although, silicon based light emitting devices may offer intense fluorescence in certain conditions, CQDs have edge in adjustment of tunability and better photostability required for long term bioimaging [20]. Whereas, PAMAM is an exceptional drug delivery nanocarrier. Thus, complex of CQDs and PAMAM dendrimers (CQDs-PAMAM complex) can serve as an efficient theranostic nanocarrier. This complex can be prepared by either chemical conjugation or ionic interaction between PAMAM dendrimer and CQDs. The covalent conjugation of dendrimer with CQDs may quench the fluorescence of CQDs, thus imaging properties of such complexes impacted negatively for biomedical application. On the other hand, complex formation through physical interaction like electrostatic forces found to be more effective in CQDs-PAMAM complex formation, which facilitates enhanced fluorescence property of CQDs through charge transfer process [21]. Thus, CQDs-PAMAM complexes were prepared via electrostatic interaction between CQDs and, full and half-generation PAMAM dendrimers.

During the literature survey, we found very few articles exploring CQDs-PAMAM complexes, for siRNA and drug delivery application as theranostic nanocarrier for treatment of various diseases [22–24]. Although these researches proved the importance of CQDs-PAMAM complexes as theranostic nanocarriers [25], however, in current research work, we have explored photophysical behaviour and stability, in presence of different salts, temperature, and pH, of CQDs-PAMAM complexes prepared with two half generation (G3.5 and G4.5) and two full generation (G4.0 and G5.0) PAMAM dendrimers. The detailed photophysical study of PAMAM dendrimer and CQDs complexes is required to establish these nanocarriers as a theranostic agent for use in various disease conditions like cancer, neurodegenerative disorders etc. Thus, the focus of this research is to study fluorescence behaviour of complexes of different full and half generation of PAMAM dendrimers with CQDs. Such theranostic nanocarriers have numbers of application in biomedical field for simultaneous diagnosis and drug delivery in various life-threatening diseases like cancer [26,27], Alzheimer [13,28], inflammation [14], etc. To the best of our knowledge this is the first study exploring complexes of half and full generation PAMAM dendrimers with CQDs to prove the potential for theranostic applications at one platform.

The main objective of this research work is to study the photophysical behaviour and stability of CQDs as well as CQDs-PAMAM complexes and constitute CQDs-PAMAM complex as potential theranostic nanocarrier. CQDs is undoubtedly a better imaging agent compared to commonly used organic dyes, whereas PAMAM dendrimers are commercially available and have been well explored for the drug delivery application by scientific community. CQDs was prepared by time efficient and industrially feasible microwave assisted pyrolysis method. The various process parameters and material attributes were evaluated individually to get the CQDs of desired size with best fluorescent efficiency, followed by the morphological analysis. The CQDs-PAMAM complexes were prepared by electrostatic interaction and evaluated by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, fluorescence quenching study (temperature, pH, and salts), photostability and storage stability study.

Citric acid, β-alanine, ethylenediamine core PAMAM dendrimers G3.5 (MW 12931 Da, 64 COOH terminal groups), G4 (MW 14215 Da, 64 NH2 terminal groups), G4.5 (MW 26251.86 Da, 64 NH2 terminal groups), G5 (MW 28826 Da, 128 NH2 terminal groups), were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, United States). Omnipore™ 0.1 µm PTFE membrane filter was purchased from Merck Millipore Ltd (Darmstadt, Germany). The Tube-o-dialyzer (1 KDa and 3.5 KDa) was purchased from the Biosciences (St. Louis, United States). The ultrapure water used throughout the experiments was acquired from an in-house Milli-Q® system (Millipore Co., USA). All other chemicals and solvents used in experiments were of analytical grade.

CQDs were prepared using citric acid as a carbon precursor and β-alanine as a passivating agent via microwave-assisted pyrolysis method [29]. A 1:2 M ratio of citric acid and β-alanine was dissolved in triple distilled water. Suitable amount of solution was transferred into the G30 vial and placed in the microwave synthesizer. The reaction was carried out at specific temperature for suitable time. The solution was dialyzed for 48 h against double distilled water using tube-o-dialyzer (1 kDa) for purification followed by lyophilization. The optimization study was performed using one factor at a time method. The details of factor and its levels are mentioned in Table 1 .

The CQDs and PAMAM dendrimers were mixed in the mass ratio of 1:4. Specifically, 0.5 mL of 2000 ppm aqueous CQDs solution was mixed with 4 mg/mL of PAMAM dendrimers (G3.5, G4.0, G4.5 and G5.0). Finally, triple distilled water was added to produce a final solution of 3 mL and left to react for 48 h at room temperature under stirring conditions to prepare CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes. The complete reaction was performed in the dark condition [22].

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM complexes were obtained using a fluorescence spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, Carry G9800A Eclipsed fluorescence spectrometer). The measurements were performed using 1 cm quartz cuvettes, with a scan speed of 500 nm/min. The PL spectra of CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM complexes were collected using a 1 mg/mL concentration with fixed excitation at 345 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of samples were measured using a particle size analyser (Nano ZS90, Malvern, UK). The hydrodynamic diameter of CQDs was analysed at 25 °C at a 173° backscattering angle. Similarly, the zeta-potential measurements were conducted using a polystyrene capillary cell (DTS1070) and analysed based on 20 trials. Additionally, three independent measurements of all the samples were taken [30].

The shape and structure of CQDs was studied using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (Hitachi H7500, Tokyo, Japan). The study was conducted at operating voltages of 100–200 kV, using a charge coupling device as the detector. A dilute drop of the sample was placed on a carbon-coated copper grid after air drying, and the dried grid containing the CQDs was examined under HRTEM. The TEM images were analysed using Image J software [30].

The surface functionalities of the CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes were analysed using an IR spectrophotometer (Bruker Alpha, UK). The prepared CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM complexes were scanned, and the spectra were obtained in the wavenumber range of 650–4000 cm−1 [31].

The effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes were investigated using a fluorescence spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, Carry G9800A Eclipsed fluorescence spectrometer). The CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM complexes were evaluated at a temperature range between 25 and 50 °C. Typically, 2000 ppm of blank CQDs and complexes were analyzed for the evaluation of excitation as well as emission spectra to study temperature dependent quenching behaviour.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on fluorescence properties of CQDs and CQDs PAMAM complexes
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and CQDs- dendrimer complexes were evaluated. Both the CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM complexes were evaluated in the pH range of 2–10. Briefly, 200 μL of a solution containing CQDs or CQDs-PAMAM complexes was added to 1800 μL of buffer solution of specific pH. Fluorescence intensity was measured using fluorescence spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, Carry G9800A Eclipsed fluorescence spectrometer).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ionic strength on fluorescence properties of CQDs and CQDs PAMAM complexes
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of ionic strength conditions on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes was carried out using NaCl and KCl as ionic agents with concentrations ranging from 0 to 200 mM (20 mM increments). CQDs or CQDs-PAMAM complexes (i.e., 100 μL) was added to the 1900 μL of ionic agents of specific molar concentrations. In the end, 200 μL of the final solution was added to a 96-well plate and fluorescence intensity was recorded using a multimode microplate reader (Synergy, Biotech) at an excitation wavelength of 345 nm.

The photostability of fluorescent CQDs and its complexes were assessed via a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer equipped with a pulsed Xenon lamp operating at an excitation wavelength of 345 nm and an emission wavelength of 420 nm. The scan duration was set at 240 min, and the excitation and emission slit widths were maintained at 10 nm. Each measurement was performed under continuous monitoring with an average time of 0.1 s.

The storage stability study of CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes were carried out in freezer conditions at −20 ± 0.5 °C for a period of 3 months. The samples fluorescence intensity was measured over the period of 3 months to observe any significant changes in intensity with respect to number of days.

All samples used in this research work were freshly prepared and the tests were performed in triplicate. The experimental results were presented as mean ± S.D. The results were statistically analysed using the Graph Pad Prism v8.0 software. In all cases, a one-way ANOVA test was employed, followed by multiple comparisons post-tests, and differences were regarded as significant only when the p value was lower than 0.05.

The CQDs were prepared by using citric acid as carbon precursor and β-alanine as passivating agent in 1:2 M ratio, via the microwave-assisted pyrolysis method. Citric acid, a natural carbon source is non-toxic, water soluble, easily oxidize and cost-effective precursor, which showed photoluminescence from blue to red regions upon formation of CQDs with high quantum yield [32]. β-alanine, a non-toxic, and water-soluble amino acid was used as a passivating agent in synthesis of CQDs, which reduces the number of surface defects and increases the quantum yield [33,34]. Citric acid and β-alanine were subjected to a temperature of 190 °C for 30 min in a microwave synthesizer reactor. The microwave reactor provides high energy microwaves, which helps in rapidly achieving the desired temperature and decompose the reaction mixture. During the process, the carbon atoms in citric acid molecule were rearranged and recombined to form small carbon clusters, which is also called carbonization. The passivating agent, (β-alanine) prevents the agglomeration of the formed carbon clusters and promotes the formation of fluorescent CQDs [35].

The effects of process parameters like reaction temperature, volume of the reaction mixture, concentration of reaction mixture and processing time were evaluated by varying one parameter at a time to examine their effect on particle size, zeta potential and fluorescence behaviour as mentioned in Fig. 1 . The batches prepared at 200 °C showed significantly (p<0.001) higher fluorescence intensity than CQDs prepared at 180 °C and 190 °C. The reduction in emission intensity at lower temperature might be due to the poor carbonization of the precursor inducing surface defects. For instance, Sangjan and co-workers have also reported similar findings indicating that at low temperatures, a prominent low fluorescence intensity was observed. However, as the temperature was increased, a significant increase in fluorescence intensity was observed due to the complete carbonization of the precursor [36]. The concentration of the reaction mixture also affected emission intensity. The highest intensity of 604.89 a.u. was observed at 300 mg/mL and it linearly decreased with decrease in the concentration of reaction mixture with linear correlation coefficient (R2) value of 0.9944. The increase in PL intensity on increasing the concentration of precursor is mainly influenced by the formation of larger amount of fluorescent CQDs. Putro et al. investigated the impact of precursor concentration on the optical properties of carbon dots (CDs) and revealed that an increase in precursor concentration resulted in a significant increase in fluorescence intensity of the synthesized CDs. This finding is attributed to the formation of a larger quantity of CQDs, arising from the increased availability of precursor material [37]. Additionally, high concentrations of CQDs dispersed in a solvent could become unstable due to the proximity of the CQDs with each other and due to high surface energy aggregation and instability was observed [38]. Thus, concentrations of the reaction mixture should be optimum.

With increase in the volume of the reaction mixture (such as from 0.5 mL to 0.75 mL, or from 0.75 mL to 1 mL) a decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed. This decrease may be attributed to reduction in the reaction rate with larger volumes of the reaction mixture, whereas a higher concentration of reactants may potentially increase the reaction rate when the volume of the reaction mixture is reduced. The change in reaction kinetic affects the generation of CQDs with optimum size and optical properties. Likewise, reaction time also affects the fluorescent intensity of CQDs. It was observed that at a reaction time of 40 min, fluorescent intensity was reduced probably due to the over-carbonization of carbon precursor and formation of insoluble black carbon particles. While in case of CQDs prepared at a reaction time of 20 min, reduced fluorescence intensity was observed due to the insufficient carbonization of carbon precursor. This result aligns with the previously reported data [39]. The optimization study of different process parameters concluded that CQDs with 0.5 mL reaction volume and 200 mg/mL reactant concentration successfully produced CQDs with an emission intensity of 339 a.u. after reaction time of 30 min at 190 °C in microwave synthesizer.

CQDs containing solution was excited at different wavelength by setting emission wavelength at 415 nm to get the excitation maxima. The detailed fluorescence behaviour of prepared CQDs was evaluated to get excitation and emission maxima. The results showed increase in the fluorescence intensity up to excitation wavelength of 345 nm and excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra is shown in Fig. 2
A with varying excitation wavelengths between 310 and 380 nm. Further increase in excitation wavelength showed a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity with red shift. Emission maxima was measured by exciting CQDs at 345 nm and spectra was recorded between 360 and 600 nm. The synthesized CQDs showed an excitation maximum of 345 nm and an emission maximum of 425 nm (Fig. 2 B). The prepared CQDs exhibited notable Stokes shift of around 80 nm, which is substantially higher than typical organic dyes such as fluorescein and rhodamine (Stokes shifts in the range of 30–50 nm for organic dyes). The CQDs having large Stokes shifts are more important for bioimaging as they show improved sensitivity as well as stability with better resistance toward photobleaching [40]. The fluorescence property of CQDs solution in day light and ultraviolet light are shown in Fig. 2C. In UV light it emits bright blue fluorescence, while in day light it looked like yellow to pale brown colour liquid.

The hydrodynamic diameter of CQDs was determined by dynamic light scattering. The obtained results revealed hydrodynamic diameter of CQDs was 9.0 ± 2.39 nm with zeta potential value −6.40 ± 0.24 mV as shown in Fig. 3
A, B and C. Transmission electron microscopy of CQDs revealed an average particle size of 12.34 ± 2.05 nm with spherical shape as shown in Fig. 3 D.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs and PAMAM dendrimer complexes
ce:section.ce:para:
PAMAM dendrimer is a hyperbranched polymer and have been widely explored by scientific community for drug delivery and scientists have found promising results in multiple animal studies with PAMAM dendrimer as drug delivery carrier. Its complex formation with CQDs provides it a theranostic potential. Theranostic systems have immense potential to change the current treatment protocol and improve the patient’s survival rate due to its ability to provide real time imaging of the diseased condition in some life-threatening diseases. Thus, we have prepared a CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes by using the charge difference on both the molecule, which is the simplest and most reliable method. Amphiphilic CQDs were allowed to stirred with full generation positively charged PAMAM dendrimers (G4.0 and G5.0) and half generation negatively charged PAMAM dendrimers (G3.5 and G4.5) to form CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes by electrostatic interaction. The fluorescence spectroscopic analysis of CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes exhibited a red shift (bathochromic shift) in comparison to blank CQDs as shown in Fig. 4
A. The blank CQDs showed a maximum emission at 425 nm, while CQDs-PAMAM G5.0, CQDs-PAMAM G4.0, CQDs-PAMAM G3.5, and CQDs-PAMAM G4.5 complexes exhibited an emission maximum at 433 nm, 436 nm, 441.96 nm and 438.93 nm respectively. The observed red shift in the emission wavelength of CQDs to the respective CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes attributed to the alteration of the electronic structure of the CQDs or change in their size due to the interaction with PAMAM dendrimers [22]. The CQDs and its complexes with full as well as half generation dendrimers gave a bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light (at excitation wavelength of 365 nm), as shown in Fig. 4 B. To further confirm the complex formation and to check the stability of complex, FTIR spectroscopy, quenching and stability studies were performed, which are discussed in following sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface group analysis of CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM complexes by FTIR spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectroscopy was conducted to characterize the different chemical functional groups on the CQDs and evaluation of CQDs-PAMAM complex formation. The FTIR spectrum of citric acid, β-alanine, CQDs, PAMAM dendrimers (G3.5, G4.5, G4.0, G5.0) and CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes are represented in Fig. 5 . FTIR spectrum of citric acid showed characteristic peaks at 1136 cm−1 and 1692 cm−1 attributed to the C-O and CO of the carboxylate group (COO–) as well as carbonyl (CO) group of carboxylic acid, respectively. Additionally, the peak observed at 3169 cm−1 is typically associated with the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl (O–H) group. FTIR spectrum of β-alanine showed a peak at 1512 cm−1 signifying the C-O of the carboxylate group (COO–), while peak at 3032 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of the N–H bond of amino group. This indicates the presence of carboxylic acid as well as amino groups in β-alanine as shown in Fig. 5 A.

After preparation of CQDs, two intense peaks were observed in FTIR spectra of CQDs, at 1176 cm−1 and 1691 cm−1, which were attributed to the presence of carboxylate groups (COO–) and carbonyl groups (CO) of carboxylic acid showing slight shift in comparison to citric acid. The successful passivation of β-alanine (incorporation into CQDs structure) was confirmed by the presence of peaks at 3162 cm−1 and 1395 cm -1, which represented the N–H stretching vibration and C-N vibrations, respectively. Similarly, Jung et al. characterized the functional groups present on the surface of CQDs and found the peaks at 1174 cm−1, 1772 cm−1 and 3317 cm−1, which were corresponding to N–H (stretching), C-O and CO vibrations, respectively [41]. Overall, the FTIR spectra of CQDs suggested the presence of carboxyl and amine groups in CQDs structure, which provides amphiphilic property to CQDs. Whereas the presence of N–H and C-N represent the successful passivation by β-alanine molecule. The passivation of nitrogen containing molecule (β-alanine) inside the CQDs structure provide enhanced fluorescence capacity to CQDs, which is helpful for deep tissue imaging during in vivo studies.

FTIR analysis of individual PAMAM dendrimers (i.e., G3.5, G4.5, G4.0 and G5.0) and their respective CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes were carried out to observe certain shifting in characteristic peaks as well as to evaluate the complex formation as shown in Fig. 5 B and 5C. The full-generation PAMAM dendrimers (G4.0 and G5.0) showed peaks around 1000 cm−1 and 3320 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of the C-N and N–H bonds due to the presence of amide bond in PAMAM dendrimers, while sharp peak at 2830 cm−1 belong to asymmetric stretching vibration of aliphatic C–H groups present in the ethylene diamine core as well as monomer of the PAMAM dendrimer. Similarly, the peaks at 1567 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of PAMAM dendrimers is typically associated with the stretching vibration of the amide (CO) bond corresponds to the carbonyl group of amide bond present in the structure of half generation dendrimers. Similarly, peak around 3320 cm−1 is corresponded to the O–H stretching of the terminal carboxyl group present in half generations PAMAM dendrimers.

The FTIR spectra of CQDs-PAMAM complexes are depicted in Fig. 5 C. We have observed the O–H peak shifting around 3200 cm−1 to 3740 cm−1 in all PAMAM dendrimers complexes due to the presence of highly electronegative molecule and ionic interaction between PAMAM dendrimers and CQDs. These results comply with the previously published report [42]. Similarly, second important peak N–H stretching of CQDs which was previously found at 3162 cm−1 shifted to around 3200 cm−1 in full generation PAMAM dendrimer complexes. While half generation PAMAM dendrimer complexes showed C-O and O–H peak shifting due to the interaction with CQDs. Overall, the peaks shifting represents the successful ionic interaction between PAMAM dendrimers with CQDs, and subsequent complex formation. Thus, the bathochromic shift in emission wavelength maxima and FTIR study combinedly prove the successful formation of CQDs-PAMAM complexes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature on fluorescence properties of CQDs and its complexes
ce:section.ce:para:
The temperature associated fluorescence property of in house formulated CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM complexes were studied between temperatures range of 20 to 60 °C. The results of the study with CQDs solution demonstrated temperature dependent fluorescence quenching behavior, without causing any change in emission maxima at performed temperature range (Fig. 6
A). CQDs showed around 30 % drop in fluorescence intensity with increase in temperature. The results indicated the significant quenching (p < 0.001) of fluorescence intensity upon stepwise increase in temperature (5 °C increment each time), due to the relaxation of photoexcited electrons with non-radiative relaxation upon the heating of CQDs solution. In a previous study, Bogireddy et al. fabricated nitrogen doped CQDs for the detection of toxic colorants, which also showed a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity upon increasing temperature attributed to the non-radiative relaxation of photoexcited electrons during the heating process. The nonradiative relaxation transfer associated with the excited electrons coming to its original state do not emit any energy in form of radiation in visible range and thus quench the fluorescence intensity of CQDs [43]. This temperature dependent fluorescence behavior could be applicable in designing temperature sensitive devices for sensing applications and measuring temperature in living cells. For instance, Wang and co-workers developed fluorescent nanohybrid with CQDs and polymer to measure the temperature of embryonic kidney cells (HEK293T cells) [44]. Although, the prepared CQDs showed the quenching effect upon increasing the temperature, however fluorescent intensity is sufficient for biomedical application like in vivo imaging and drug delivery.

Similarly, temperature quenching studies of CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes were also evaluated using fluorescence spectrometer between temperature range 20–60 °C as shown in Fig. 6 B to 6E. The significant quenching in fluorescent intensity of the CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes with the increase in temperature was observed. This observation might be due to the increase in thermal energy of CQDs, which causes molecules to vibrate and rotate more vigorously leading to non-radiative relaxation of photoexcited electrons. Similar results were obtained by Guo and coworkers, where scientists observed 46 % decrease in fluorescence intensity of nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon dots at a temperature range of 10 °C to 70 °C [45]. The CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes with full-generation PAMAM dendrimers showed around 40 % drop in fluorescence intensity ( Fig. 6 D and E), whereas complexes with half generation PAMAM dendrimers showed around a 35 % drop in fluorescence intensity within temperature range of 20–60 °C ( Fig. 6 B and C). Comparatively, the larger drop in fluorescence intensity with full generations PAMAM dendrimers as compared to half generation PAMAM dendrimers represents comparatively higher binding of CQDs with negatively charged half generation PAMAM dendrimers compared to positively charged full generation PAMAM dendrimers. Further, the larger dendrimers complex produces more significant steric hindrance and tighter binding, leading to a higher degree of fluorescence quenching, for instance half generation dendrimer showed 1.44 % more quenching with higher generation. Thus, the decrease in fluorescence intensity in CQDs-PAMAM complex varies with increase in PAMAM generation, which is in alignment with previously reported results by Ghosh and co-workers [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on fluorescence behavior and stability of CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence behaviour of CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM complexes was also measured at pH range of 2–10. The pH of a biological system is an important parameter, which need to be considered in the development of nanocarriers. pH plays a crucial role in the stability of nanocarriers; thus, it is necessary to check pH stability of nanocarrier. Here, the pH stability of CQDs and its complexes were checked by measuring the fluorescence intensity. Any potential aggregation of theranostic nanocomplexes were noted by drastic change in fluorescence intensity. This study will be helpful to establish reliability, performance, and versatility of CQDs and its complexes in a wide range of biomedical applications, including bioimaging, biosensing, and in drug delivery. It was observed that CQDs showed the highest fluorescence intensity at basic pH of 8–10, while at acidic pH levels, specifically pH 2 and pH 3, a reduction in fluorescence intensity i.e., a hypochromic shift was observed by a slight shift in emission wavelengths towards shorter wavelengths (Fig. 7 and figure S1 ). This is mainly due to the protonation of carboxyl groups at lower pH which leads to the aggregation of CQDs causing quenching effect. Yang and co-workers developed nitrogen-doped carbon dots and reported a decrease in photoluminescence intensity at acidic pH due to the aggregation caused by the carboxyl acid at lower pH [46]. On the other hand, the CQDs-PAMAM complexes showed highest fluorescence intensity in an acidic condition with red shift in emission maxima. Whereas in alkaline conditions a significant (p < 0.01) reduction in fluorescence intensity was observed. This behavior is mainly due to the protonation of terminal amino groups in case of full generations and deprotonation of carboxyl groups in case of half generation PAMAM dendrimers in acidic environment as well as deprotonation under alkaline conditions [11,47]. The comparative decrease in the fluorescent intensity is higher at alkaline pH in lower generation PAMAM dendrimer (G3.5 and G4.0)-CQDs complexes compared to its subsequent higher generation PAMAM dendrimer (G4.5 and G5.0)-CQDs complexes. Thus, we can say that CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes emits more intense fluorescence at acidic pH. Further, a slight increase in the fluorescence intensity was observed at pH 3 in half generation PAMAM dendrimer–CQDs complexes, while full generation PAMAM dendrimer–CQDs complexes showed similar observation at pH 8 due to lesser ionization of carboxyl and amine groups at acidic and basic pH, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ionic strength on CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM complexes
ce:section.ce:para:
CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes were evaluated in the presence of different salt concentrations to understand the effect of salt/ions on fluorescence behavior of CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes. As these nano complexes are intended to be used as theranostic carriers and administered into the biological environment, hence it is necessary for these complexes to retain their original fluorescence property in the presence of commonly found ions in biological systems. The effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of synthesized CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM complexes were evaluated using different ionic strengths of NaCl and KCl as both are the major ions present in the body. The salt concentrations were prepared from 0 to 200 mM with increments of 20 mM and fluorescence intensity was measured. CQDs showed 5–15 % and 10–30 % decrease in fluorescence quenching in presence of NaCl and KCl salts, respectively as shown in Fig. 8
(A) and 8(B). Complexes of CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer also showed decrease in emission intensity with increasing the concentration of NaCl and KCl, but it was comparatively lesser than the CQDs alone. However, there was a 5–15 % drop in fluorescence intensity was observed at highest salt concentrations as shown in Fig. 8 (A) and 8(B). The fluorescent quenching is slightly higher in presence of KCl compared to NaCl. Further, we have not found any notable difference in fluorescence quenching among various CQDs – dendrimer complexes.

The comparatively stable fluorescence of complexes was due to the presence of PAMAM dendrimer, which protected CQDs from quenching under high salt concentration by decreasing the electronic exchange [48]. This study indicates the stability of complexes at varied ionic environment which is crucial for its practical applications as theranostic agent. Further, both half generation and full generation dendrimers showed equivalent efficacy in protecting CQDs from quenching under high salt concentration showing that both type of complexes can be used successfully for theranostic applications.

Fluorescent materials like CQDs undergo photobleaching under presence of ultraviolet light, which is described as irreversible loss of fluorescence intensity and loss of optical properties. Thus, photostability study is important to perform for the compounds intended as imaging agent for biomedical application. These CQDs might be used in bioimaging or as dye for cellular staining due to its better photostability compared to other dyes [49]. The photostability study was carried out to investigate the ability of CQDs and its complexes with dendrimer to sustain against photochemical degradation under continuous irradiation of UV light for a duration of 240 min. The results showed that the CQDs alone showed negligible fluorescence decay (0.70 %) after being subjected to ultraviolet irradiation for the entire 240 min as depicted in Fig. 9 . Whereas, in case of CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes, a slight decrease (3 to 3.5 %) in the fluorescence intensity was observed. Additionally, the full generation PAMAM dendrimers showed more decrease in fluorescence intensity with increasing generation from G4.0 to G5.0 due to the structural differences. On the other hand, such differences were negligible with half generation PAMAM dendrimer complexes. These findings suggested that the prepared CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM complexes exhibited a noticeable degree of resistance towards photobleaching and could retain its optical properties under UV exposure. Similarly in a previous research, Shi and co-workers have prepared red emissive carbon dots and compared its photostability with Rhodamine 6G. The results of photostability study showed 6 % decrease in their fluorescence intensity of carbon dots, while Rhodamine 6G showed a 26.7 % decay in fluorescence within 60 min of ultraviolet irradiation [50].

Conclusively, CQDs and CQDs-PAMAM dendrimer complexes have shown excellent photostability under UV irradiation and could contribute more potentially in theranostic application compared to commonly used dyes like Rhodamine 6G.

The CQDs were prepared via a microwave-assisted synthesis method using citric acid and β-alanine (1:2 M ratio) as the carbon source and passivating agent, respectively. CQDs has particle size of 12.34 ± 2.05 nm and a zeta potential of −6.40 ± 0.24 mV. Further, CQDs-PAMAM complexes were prepared with 1:4 mass ratio of CQDs and PAMAM dendrimers. The fluorescence spectroscopy analysis of CQDs-PAMAM complexes exhibited a red shift in emission wavelength indicating the successful complex formation, which was further confirmed by peak shifting in FTIR spectra of CQDs-PAMAM complexes. Additionally, CQDs and CQD-PAMAM complexes showed pH dependent alteration and temperature dependent quenching in fluorescence intensity, however it was within the limit and suitable for biomedical applications. Along with this, CQDs-PAMAM complexes also showed higher fluorescence intensity at pH around 5, which could facilitate its potential in theranostic application in various diseases including cancer. The evaluation of CQDs-PAMAM complexes under varied ionic conditions showed good stability with potential to be used under complex physiological conditions as theranostic agent. At last, photostability study do not show any significant photobleaching up to 4 hrs and storage stability provide stable fluorescence upto 2 months. Conclusively, the CQDs-PAMAM complexes can serve as an efficient nanotheranostic carrier due to its stability in various pH, temperature, salt conditions as well as its resistance towards photobleaching.

Stanzin Sonam: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Parth Patel: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Keerti Jain: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Department of Pharmaceuticals, Ministry of Chemicals and Fertilizers, Government of India for providing facilities to carry out this research work. The Authors also acknowledge SAIF, Panjab University for HRTEM analysis. The NIPER-R communication number for this manuscript is NIPER-R/Communication/453.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optlastec.2024.110761.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
